section.title: The Exodus from Egypt
section.sectionRationale: This is the hinge of the whole book: the plague falls exactly as foretold, Pharaoh finally lets Israel go, and after four hundred and thirty years of sojourning a mixed multitude walks free. The Passover statute is then fixed for every generation to come.
section.thesis: At midnight the LORD strikes Egypt's firstborn and Pharaoh drives Israel out; the people leave in haste, plundering Egypt as promised, and the LORD seals the Passover as a lasting statute for native and foreigner alike who join themselves to Him.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the midnight judgment and Pharaoh's urgent release of Israel; the actual departure, the plunder, and the note on the four hundred and thirty years; and the closing statutes fixing who may keep the Passover and how.

units.label: Midnight, and Pharaoh releases Israel
units.note: the cry goes up, the sentence executed

verses.text: Now at midnight the LORD struck down every firstborn male in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharaoh, who sat on his throne, to the firstborn of the prisoner in the dungeon, as well as all the firstborn among the livestock.
During the night Pharaoh got up, he and all his officials and all the Egyptians, and there was loud wailing in Egypt; for there was no house without someone dead.
Then Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron by night and said, “Get up, leave my people, both you and the Israelites! Go, worship the LORD as you have requested.
Take your flocks and herds as well, just as you have said, and depart! And bless me also.”
And in order to send them out of the land quickly, the Egyptians urged the people on. “For otherwise,” they said, “we are all going to die!”

units.label: The departure and the plunder
units.note: 430 years, and the LORD's divisions go out

verses.text: So the people took their dough before it was leavened, carrying it on their shoulders in kneading bowls wrapped in clothing.
Furthermore, the Israelites acted on Moses' word and asked the Egyptians for articles of silver and gold, and for clothing.
And the LORD gave the people such favor in the sight of the Egyptians that they granted their request. In this way they plundered the Egyptians.
The Israelites journeyed from Rameses to Succoth with about 600,000 men on foot, besides women and children.
And a {{mixed|mixed}} multitude also went up with them, along with great droves of livestock, both flocks and herds.

Since their dough had no leaven, the people baked what they had brought out of Egypt into unleavened loaves. For when they had been driven out of Egypt, they could not delay and had not prepared any provisions for themselves.
Now the duration of the Israelites' stay in Egypt was 430 years.
At the end of the 430 years, to the very day, all the LORD's divisions went out of the land of Egypt.

Because the LORD kept a {{vigil|vigil}} that night to bring them out of the land of Egypt, this same night is to be a vigil to the LORD, to be observed by all the Israelites for the generations to come.

units.label: Statutes for the Passover
units.note: who may eat it, and how

verses.text: And the LORD said to Moses and Aaron, “This is the statute of the Passover: No foreigner is to eat of it.
But any slave who has been purchased may eat of it, after you have circumcised him.
A temporary resident or hired hand shall not eat the Passover.
It must be eaten inside one house. You are not to take any of the meat outside the house, and you may not break any of the bones.
The whole congregation of Israel must celebrate it.

If a {{foreigner|foreigner}} resides with you and wants to celebrate the LORD's Passover, all the males in the household must be circumcised; then he may come near to celebrate it, and he shall be like a native of the land. But no uncircumcised man may eat of it.
The same law shall apply to both the native and the foreigner who resides among you.”
Then all the Israelites did this, they did just as the LORD had commanded Moses and Aaron.
And on that very day the LORD brought the Israelites out of the land of Egypt by their divisions.

mixed.gloss: A mixed multitude, a great mixture of non-Israelites who left Egypt along with Israel. From the very night of the exodus, God's people include those grafted in from outside, foreshadowing a redeemed people not limited to physical descent from Abraham.

vigil.gloss: A night of watching, guarding, keeping vigil. The LORD Himself kept watch that night to bring Israel out, and Israel is then called to keep the same night as an act of watchful remembrance every year.

foreigner.gloss: A resident foreigner living among Israel. The law makes room for such a person to join the covenant people fully through circumcision, showing that the boundary of Israel was never simply ethnic but covenantal, open to whoever would be joined to the LORD.

basic.context: This is the night Israel actually leaves Egypt. The plague falls exactly as warned, Pharaoh finally breaks and lets the people go, and Israel walks out with plunder from the very people who enslaved them, four hundred and thirty years after they first went down to Egypt.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes a real historical departure at a fixed point in time; it is not a template for how to treat employers or neighbors today. The rules about who may eat the Passover describe Israel's specific covenant boundary, later opened fully in Christ to all nations without requiring circumcision, Galatians 3.
basic.originalLanguages: A mixed multitude, erev rav, leaves Egypt alongside Israel, hinting that God's people were never purely one ethnic line. The LORD keeps shimurim, a vigil, that night, and calls Israel to keep the same watch every year after. And the ger, the resident foreigner, could be joined fully to Israel through circumcision, on the same terms as any native.
basic.typology: The four hundred and thirty years ending to the very day shows God's precision in keeping His promises on His own timetable. The mixed multitude leaving with Israel anticipates a people of God gathered from every nation, not limited to physical descent, fulfilled in the church, Galatians 3:28-29.
basic.christ: The unbroken bones of the Passover lamb, fixed as law in verse 46, are fulfilled exactly at the cross, John 19:36, where Jesus' legs are not broken though the other two crucified beside Him have theirs broken. The precise timing of deliverance points to a God who keeps every promise to the day, as He will keep His promise of Christ's return.
basic.god: God is exactingly faithful, bringing Israel out on the very day the 430 years were complete, and He is also generous, opening the door of covenant belonging to any foreigner willing to be joined to His people through the appointed sign. Judgment and welcome sit side by side in this chapter.
basic.crossReferences: Galatians 3:16, 3:28-29 speak of a mixed people, Jew and Gentile, united in Christ, the true seed of Abraham. John 19:36 fulfills the unbroken bones of the Passover lamb. Genesis 15:13-14 had already foretold 400 years of affliction, fulfilled here precisely.
basic.oneStory: God keeps His ancient promise to the day, judges the oppressor, and brings out a people that already includes those from beyond Israel's bloodline. This is the pattern the whole Bible completes: a people gathered from every nation, redeemed by a lamb, walking out of bondage into freedom in Christ.

context.literary: The narrative moves at speed here, midnight judgment, immediate expulsion, hurried departure, then slows to give exact numbers and a fixed timespan before closing with detailed statutes on who may share in the Passover meal.
context.historical: The 430 years anchors Israel's sojourn in Egypt to a specific, calculable span, matching the promise given to Abraham in Genesis 15:13, and the plunder of Egypt fulfills what God had told Moses back at the burning bush, Exodus 3:21-22.
context.canonical: This chapter establishes Passover's legal boundaries for all later Israelite practice and sets the pattern, God keeping covenant promises with exact faithfulness, that runs through the rest of the biblical story to its fulfillment in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the literal, historical departure from Egypt as the fulfillment of God's ancient promise to Abraham, and to fix the terms of the Passover as a permanent, covenantal ordinance with clear boundaries.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates a specific historical deliverance and prescribes Passover's terms for Israel under the old covenant; it does not directly legislate immigration policy or employer-employee relations, though its concern for the ger, the resident foreigner, reflects an enduring principle of welcome toward outsiders who seek to belong to God's people.

debates.question: What is the significance of the mixed multitude leaving with Israel?

views.label: A picture of eventual inclusion of the nations
views.case: Even at the founding exodus, non-Israelites join God's redeemed people, anticipating the later invitation for foreigners to be circumcised and belong, verses 48 to 49, and ultimately the ingathering of the nations in Christ.

views.label: A source of later trouble
views.case: Numbers 11:4 traces the rabble's craving back to this mixed group, suggesting their presence, while not condemned here, later contributed to Israel's grumbling in the wilderness.

debates.bearing: Both readings can be held together: inclusion of outsiders is a genuine grace, yet belonging to God's people always calls for full covenant commitment, not mere proximity.

study.originalLanguages: The erev rav, the mixed multitude, leaves Egypt alongside ethnic Israel, a detail Scripture neither hides nor dwells on, but one that matters for how God's people are later defined. The LORD's shimurim, His night of watching, becomes Israel's own night of watching, kept every year. And the ger, the resident foreigner, is not locked out of the Passover but invited in on the same terms as a native, through circumcision.

typology.type: The unbroken bones of the Passover lamb, verse 46
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled precisely at the cross when Jesus' legs are not broken, John 19:33, 36.

typology.type: The mixed multitude joining Israel's exodus
typology.fulfillment: A picture of Jew and Gentile united as one people in Christ, Galatians 3:28-29, Ephesians 2:11-16.

typology.type: 430 years fulfilled to the very day
typology.fulfillment: God's precise faithfulness to His timetable, a pattern culminating in Christ coming in the fullness of time, Galatians 4:4.

study.christ: The law that no bone of the Passover lamb be broken, verse 46, is fulfilled with striking precision at the cross, John 19:36, tying Jesus directly to this ordinance. The exodus itself, God's people brought out of slavery by a substitute's blood, is the Old Testament's clearest rehearsal of the greater exodus Christ accomplishes at Calvary, delivering His people from a harder slavery than Egypt's, Luke 9:31.
study.god: God is faithful down to the day, fulfilling a 430-year-old promise exactly on schedule, and He is a God who welcomes outsiders into covenant belonging on His own generous terms, not excluding the ger but requiring the same commitment He asks of the native born. His justice on Egypt and His welcome to the foreigner both flow from His character.

crossReferences.note: God had foretold four hundred years of affliction followed by deliverance with great possessions, fulfilled here.
The unbroken bones of the Passover lamb are fulfilled literally in Jesus at the cross.
In Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek, all one, the true seed of Abraham, echoing the mixed multitude here.
The rabble among the Israelites, likely the mixed multitude, later stirs craving and complaint in the wilderness.

study.oneStory: God brings His ancient promise to pass on the exact day appointed, judging the oppressor and freeing a people that already includes those grafted in from outside Israel. This same pattern, precise faithfulness, judgment on sin, and a people gathered beyond one bloodline, comes to its fullness in Christ, who leads a greater exodus and gathers one people from every nation.

quiz.q: What finally moved Pharaoh to let Israel go?

options.text: The death of every firstborn in Egypt at midnight, including his own household.
options.note: Verses 29 to 31 show this was the decisive, terrible blow.

options.text: A personal request from Moses' father-in-law.
options.note: Jethro plays no role in this chapter; the tenth plague is what breaks Pharaoh.

options.text: Pharaoh had a change of heart on his own, unrelated to any plague.
options.note: The text ties his release directly to the plague of the firstborn, not an independent change of heart.

options.text: The Israelites overpowered the Egyptian army.
options.note: There is no battle here; Pharaoh releases Israel because of the plague, not military defeat.

quiz.q: How long had Israel's stay in Egypt lasted, according to verse 40?

options.text: 430 years, ending to the very day.
options.note: The text is precise about both the length and the exactness of its fulfillment.

options.text: 40 years.
options.note: 40 years describes the later wilderness wandering, not the Egyptian sojourn.

options.text: 70 years.
options.note: 70 is the number of Jacob's household that first went down to Egypt, Exodus 1:5, not the length of the stay.

options.text: The text gives no length of time.
options.note: Verse 40 states the figure plainly, 430 years.

quiz.q: Who left Egypt alongside the Israelites, according to verse 38?

options.text: A mixed multitude of non-Israelites, along with great droves of livestock.
options.note: The text explicitly notes this mixed group leaving with Israel.

options.text: Only ethnic Israelites, with no outsiders at all.
options.note: The text specifically mentions a mixed multitude joining the departure.

options.text: Pharaoh's own army, escorting them to the border.
options.note: There is no escort by Pharaoh's army; Egypt urged them to leave quickly out of fear.

options.text: No livestock, only people.
options.note: Great droves of both flocks and herds are explicitly mentioned leaving with the people.

quiz.q: Under what condition could a foreigner join in eating the Passover, according to verses 48 to 49?

options.text: All the males in his household had to be circumcised, making him like a native of the land.
options.note: This is the exact condition given, opening full covenant participation to any who would be circumcised.

options.text: No foreigner could ever eat the Passover under any circumstance.
options.note: The text explicitly allows for a foreigner to join through circumcision, contradicting a total exclusion.

options.text: Only payment of a fee was required.
options.note: No fee is mentioned; the requirement is circumcision, joining the covenant sign.

options.text: Any visitor, regardless of status, could freely eat it.
options.note: Verse 45 explicitly excludes a mere temporary resident or hired hand from eating it without the covenant sign.

quiz.q: What detail of the Passover lamb in this chapter is later fulfilled at the cross?

options.text: That none of its bones would be broken.
options.note: Verse 46 is fulfilled literally in Jesus at the cross, John 19:36.

options.text: That it must be eaten with bitter herbs.
options.note: True of the lamb generally, but this is not the detail the New Testament singles out as fulfilled at the cross.

options.text: That it had to be a red heifer.
options.note: The red heifer is a different, unrelated ordinance in Numbers 19.

options.text: No detail from this chapter is echoed in the New Testament.
options.note: John 19:36 explicitly cites the unbroken bones from this Passover instruction.

questions: God kept His 430-year-old promise to the very day. Where do you need to trust His timing on a promise that feels long overdue?

Egypt's own goods went out with Israel as a form of restitution for years of forced labor. Where might God be calling you to trust Him for provision rather than anxiously grasping for security yourself?

A mixed multitude joined Israel's exodus from the very start. How does it change your view of the church to know that God's people have never been defined by ethnicity or background, but by belonging to Him?

The Passover lamb's unbroken bones point straight to Jesus at the cross. Where do small, seemingly minor details of your life might God also be weaving into a larger, faithful pattern you cannot yet see?

The door to the Passover was open to any foreigner willing to be joined fully to God's people. Who around you needs that same invitation into full belonging in Christ, and are you willing to extend it?

translationNotes.note: 600,000 men on foot likely refers to able-bodied men eligible for military service, meaning the total number of people, including women and children, would have been considerably larger.

The 430 years is counted differently across ancient traditions, some measuring from Jacob's descent into Egypt and others from Abraham's earlier sojourning; the text's own emphasis is on precision and fulfillment, not the details of the calculation.
Not breaking any of its bones may reflect ordinary care in preparing and eating the animal whole, but it becomes theologically loaded once John ties it directly to Jesus at the crucifixion.
